summary: Dual-rubric LLM judge with coordination-aware and standalone quality scoring
context: Analysis revealed LLM judge rubrics were biased toward coordination — baseline conditions structurally couldn't achieve "Excellent" scores since rubrics rewarded inter-agent alignment.
rationale: Adding 4 standalone quality templates (correctness, architectural soundness, maintainability, completeness) that evaluate output agnostically lets us separate "did coordination help?" from "is the output good?" — the coordinationLift metric (coordination score minus standalone score) directly measures Twining's value add.

alternatives.option: Rewrite existing rubrics to be outcome-focused only
alternatives.reason_rejected: Loses the ability to measure coordination-specific benefits — we need both dimensions to understand where Twining helps vs where standalone quality is sufficient
